ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic rice noodle class (FOODON_03311069); no specific Kueh Teow / flat rice noodle entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers rice noodles as a category. No specific flat rice noodle / kueh teow code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for kueh teow or flat rice noodles identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of kueh teow as a noodle dish in Singapore is approximately 200 g of cooked flat rice noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Rice noodles, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20065)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked rice noodles (NDB 20065 / FDC ID 169726) used as the primary nutrient reference for plain cooked kueh teow. Values represent plain cooked flat rice noodles without added sauces, oil, or protein toppings, consistent with the base ingredient. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a standalone plain kueh teow entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this staple ingredient.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Rice noodles, dried, boiled (Atkinson et al., 2008)
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 61 (medium) is sourced from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Rice noodles (dried, boiled) GI=61 is used as the closest analogous entry for plain cooked kueh teow (flat rice noodles). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 48.0) / 100 = 29.3, based on 200 g serving containing 48 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content in plain form
hypertension.guidance: Plain cooked kueh teow contains approximately 33 mg sodium per 100 g, making it a low-sodium staple; however, individuals with hypertension should be cautious of high-sodium sauces, soy sauce, and seasonings commonly added during preparation of dishes such as char kway teow.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index and high carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kueh teow has a medium GI of approximately 61 and provides around 24 g carbohydrate per 100 g; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat and negligible saturated fat in plain form
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain kueh teow is very low in fat (0.2 g per 100 g) and contains negligible saturated fat, making it a heart-friendly carbohydrate base when consumed without high-fat sauces or lard, which are common in stir-fried preparations such as char kway teow.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kueh Teow (粿条) are flat, wide rice noodles made from rice flour and water, originating from the Teochew and Hokkien communities of southern China and widely adopted across Singapore, Malaysia, and Southeast Asia. The noodles are typically steamed or boiled and have a smooth, silky, slightly chewy texture with a mild, neutral flavour that readily absorbs sauces and seasonings. In Singapore, kueh teow is most commonly encountered as the noodle base in dishes such as Char Kway Teow (stir-fried flat rice noodles with soy sauce, eggs, cockles, and bean sprouts) and Kway Teow Soup (flat rice noodles in a clear or savoury broth). As a plain cooked staple, kueh teow provides approximately 109 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrate as the dominant macronutrient (24 g per 100 g), very low fat (0.2 g), and modest protein (1.8 g). It is naturally gluten-free and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets in its plain form. The medium glycemic index (GI ≈ 61) means that portion control is advisable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The sodium content of plain kueh teow is low, but prepared dishes incorporating soy sauce, oyster sauce, or preserved ingredients can be significantly higher in sodium, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Kueh teow is a culturally significant staple in Singaporean hawker culture and is listed as part of the broader hawker food heritage.

confidence_reason: No standalone plain kueh teow entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked rice noodles (FDC ID 169726), which is the closest approved source for this staple. GI is estimated from the closest analogous entry in the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables. Confidence is medium due to source adaptation rather than an exact Singapore-specific match.
notes: Nutrient values represent plain cooked kueh teow without added sauces, oil, or protein toppings. Actual nutritional content of prepared dishes (e.g., Char Kway Teow) will differ substantially due to added fats, proteins, and sodium. saturated_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for plain cooked rice noodles; naturally zero given the absence of animal fats or added oils in the plain product. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based grain product. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked rice noodles. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero for a plant-based grain product; not reported in USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_e_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked rice noodles. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169726. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as USDA SR Legacy provides values for all listed nutrients for cooked rice noodles.
